coredata.dc:title: White light emission of carbon dots by creating different emissive traps
coredata.dc:creator: Joseph, Julin
Anappara, Aji A.
coredata.dc:description: Here we report a facile and rapid synthetic strategy for white light emitting carbon dots (CDs) by creating inhomogeneity in the surface-moieties by carbonizing ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) and ethylene glycol (EG) which are having different functional groups. The aqueous solution of the as-synthesised nanoparticles exhibits broad-band emission at several excitation wavelengths, with CIE parameters in the white gamut. Furthermore, white light emission is demonstrated through remote-phosphor technology, by capping 365nm UV chip with PMMA, after dispersing the polymer with CDs. The resulting emission from the white-LED reported colour parameters such as CIE (0.34, 0.38), CRI of 84 and CCT of 5078K.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of carbon dots as phosphors in white light emitting diodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A Ph.D. grant to Julin Joseph is acknowledged to National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC) . The authors thank to Dr. Lakshmi C. (Dept. of Chemistry, NITC) for providing facilities for fluorescence measurements, and Dr. P. Parameswaran for fruitful discussions. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-prepared carbon quantum dots. The inset shows high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of an individual carbon dot indicating the typical lattice constant. (b) Size-distribution of the CDs as obtained from the TEM. (c) The morphology of randomly chosen carbon dots as shown in AFM image. (d) The height profiles of the carbon dots obtained from the AFM.
(a) XPS survey scan spectra of the carbon dots. Inset contains high resolution N1s spectra. (b) ATR-FTIR spectra of carbon dots.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersion of carbon dots. (b) The photoluminescence spectra obtained by exciting the sample at different excitation. (c) CIE 1931chromaticity diagram showing the emission colour coordinates of the aqueous-dispersion of CDs at an excitation wavelength of 370nm. (d) The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of carbon dots at 490nm emission, obtained using TCSPC technique (with an excitation wavelength of about 340nm).

(a) The emission spectrum of the white LED as obtained using a fibre-coupled spectrophotometer. The inset of shows the digital photograph of white LED under forward biasing conditions. (b) The chromaticity diagram for the white LED, with the CIE parameters represented by the black dot.

head.ce:title: White light emission of carbon dots by creating different emissive traps

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC), Kerala 673601, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report a facile and rapid synthetic strategy for white light emitting carbon dots (CDs) by creating inhomogeneity in the surface-moieties by carbonizing ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) and ethylene glycol (EG) which are having different functional groups. The aqueous solution of the as-synthesised nanoparticles exhibits broad-band emission at several excitation wavelengths, with CIE parameters in the white gamut. Furthermore, white light emission is demonstrated through remote-phosphor technology, by capping 365nm UV chip with PMMA, after dispersing the polymer with CDs. The resulting emission from the white-LED reported colour parameters such as CIE (0.34, 0.38), CRI of 84 and CCT of 5078K.

ce:section.ce:para: The materials having broad-emission which cover a significant region of visible spectra will appear as white light for human eye. White light emitting (WLE) materials are interesting in solid-state lighting because of their use in flat panel displays and light emitting diodes [1–5]. A variety of WLE materials based on organic, polymer, rare-earth based inorganic and semiconductor quantum-dot based materials have been reported [6–8]. Typically white light emission is based on photoluminescence (optical excitation), electroluminescence (stimulated by electric field), magnetic-induced luminescence (stimulated by magnetic field) or mechanoluminescence (induced due to mechanical deformations) [9–13]. A typical route to generate white light emitting materials is by mixing different components that emit primary colours such as red, green and blue or by combining complementary colour pairs, like blue and yellow in a single device [14–17]. But the difficulty in combining the components and the interaction between them such as self-absorption in the case of quantum-dot based systems and the energy-transfer mechanisms between the components in organic materials affect the purity of white light emission [18,19]. Furthermore, the inorganic WLE materials based on rare-earth elements are expensive; meanwhile the semiconductor quantum-dot-based systems are highly toxic [20–23]. Another approach to realise white light emission is remote-phosphor technology i.e., by using an optical source such as UV or blue LED which could excite different phosphors capable enough to emit entire visible range [24,25]. But on combining blue LED with yellow-emitting phosphors, the blue light from the LED chip have higher contribution than the yellow emission from the phosphors, resulting in poor colour rendering index (CRI) and higher correlated colour temperature (CCT) parameters [26]. This issue can be solved by employing direct WLE materials in combination with UV-LEDs. Hence it is necessary to develop single-component, non-toxic, direct white light emitting materials which could be synthesised through low-cost methods.

Recently carbon dots (CDs) have been noticed as an excellent fluorescent material because of its properties such as low-toxicity, bio-compatibility, photostability, and low-cost production routes [27–31]. CDs are basically quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles having sp2 conjugated graphitic core and surface-functionalised with different functional groups. The aqueous-dispersion of carbon dots exhibit UV absorption peaks corresponding to π–π* transition in the core and n–π* transition of non-binding electron orbitals in the functional groups [32]. CDs are either produced using top-down methods from bulk carbon materials such as graphite, carbon rods, carbon fibre, carbon nanotubes, carbon black, candle-soot etc. or using bottom-up techniques using carbon-rich molecular precursors [27–31]. Typically the carbon dots synthesised through these methods emit with maximum intensity in bluish-green region, and on further excitation the emission is shifted to near IR region with narrow bandwidth [33]. In order to use CDs as a direct white light phosphor its emission should cover a significant fraction of red, green and blue regions of the visible spectrum at single-wavelength excitation.

Even though there is no unique explanation for the photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots, it is widely accepted that the UV-absorption by the CDs results from the inter-band electronic excitations resulting from the confined sp2 conjugation in the carbon core, and the fluorescence result from the surface states [34]. The emissive surface states can be either directly excited or through the energy-transfer from the intrinsic band. In other words, the nano-graphitic carbon core plays the role of a reservoir for the photo-generated carriers which then leak to the possible emissive sites (surface-states, formed by the hybridisation structure of the carbon backbone and the functional groups) [32]. Hence multi-colour emission can be realized from CDs by manipulating the surface-functional groups. Recent reports demonstrated that the emission peaks of CDs can be shifted to longer wavelengths by heteroatom doping, especially with nitrogen-doping [35–37]. The doping of nitrogen in the graphitic core enhances the fluorescence quantum-yield, as it introduces emissive traps within the core [34]. The contribution of red colour in the emission spectral composition can be enhanced by increasing the surface-oxidation and also by introducing nitrogen-containing surface-functional groups on the CDs. The generation of direct white light emission from CDs may be possible by synergistically introducing inhomogeneity in the surface-functional groups and by the shifting of the emission to longer wavelengths through nitrogen doping.

Bottom-up synthesis of CDs is usually done through the pyrolytic treatment of carbon-rich molecular precursors such as carbohydrates, carboxylic acids, glycols, polyacrylamide, gelatine, bovine albumin or natural sources such as soy milk, fruit juices etc., following hydrothermal/solvothermal, enhanced solvothermal (microwave–solvothermal, plasma–solvothermal), microwave-assisted carbonisation, ultrasonic treatment, pyrolysis in concentrated acids or combustion methods [27–31]. Even though ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) and ethylene glycol (EG) have been reported as individual precursors for the production of carbon dots, the emissions from the resulting CDs were observed in the bluish-green region, with narrow bandwidths [38,39]. Here, we are demonstrating broad-band emission from CDs by creating inhomogeneous functional groups or emissive-traps on the nanoparticles in a single-pot synthesis. Since the functional groups on the carbon nanoparticles originate from the precursors, we have selected two different precursors EDTA and EG which have COOH, C–N–C, CH2 and OH bonds. During the synthesis EDTA and EG get esterified to form a polymer-type ester under microwave irradiation (in the presence of the acid catalyst, H3PO4), and the unreacted EDTA, EG and polymer-type ester get carbonized to form carbon quantum dots with multi-functional groups. The ensemble emissions of the CDs appear as broad-band; under single-wavelength UV excitation. The presences of different emissive sites are confirmed by XPS, FTIR and time resolved spectroscopy (TCSPC).

Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylene glycol (EG), and orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Merck Millipore. Dialysis tubing having molecular weight cut-off 1kDa was supplied by Spectrum Laboratories (USA). The ultrapure water was provided from Labostar TWF water purification system (18.2MΩ Siemens ultrapure water systems) and was used for all experiments.

Carbon dots were prepared through microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment 0.2g EDTA was dissolved in 0.5mL of 0.75M NaOH solution; followed by 1mL of EG and 10mL H3PO4 under stirring conditions. The solution was then heated in Anton-Paar Monowave 300 microwave synthesis reactor at 180°C for 5min. Upon microwave irradiation the solution changed from colourless to dark brown, which was further dispersed in water and then centrifuged at 13,000rpm for 30min to remove bulk counterparts. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against ultrapure water for two days, using dialysis membrane having molecular weight cut-off 1kDa for further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on JEOL, JEM-2100F (Japan). The elemental compositions of the nanoparticles were obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, XPS (PHI-5000 Versa Probe ΙΙ). The surface functional groups of the C-dots were studied using Perkin Elmer Spectra two FTIR spectrometer in ATR mode with a resolution of about 4cm−1. The Raman spectrum was recorded using AFM-Raman integration system alfa300 RA (WiTec) by irradiating the samples using 532nm laser. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer and steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The luminescence life-time measurements were performed using a Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France) using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The emission of white LED was obtained using a fibre-coupled spectrometer (USB4000, Ocean Optics, USA).

The samples for TEM were prepared by drop-casting aqueous dispersion of nanoparticles onto a copper grid and dried under IR lamp. The AFM samples were prepared by drop-casting aqueous nanoparticle solution on a mica sheet. Powder samples were used for FTIR Raman and XPS measurements. The spectrophotometric and fluorescence studies were done after dispersing the nanoparticles in ultrapure water. The concentration of the nanoparticles in the aqueous solution used for the optical studies was estimated to be about 30μg/mL.

The surface-morphological studies of the as-synthesised nanoparticles were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). TEM image reported in Fig. 1a reveals that the nanoparticles are well dispersed and are quasi-spherical in nature. A representative HRTEM image of the nanoparticles is depicted in the inset of Fig. 1a, shows the crystalline nature of the nanoparticles with a lattice spacing of 0.30nm. The statistical particle-size distribution is calculated using 65 nanoparticles shows an average size of the nanoparticles around 4.8nm (Fig. 1b). AFM image of the nanoparticles in Fig. 1c shows the quasi-spherical nature and the height profile indicates that average height of the nanoparticles is about 2nm (Fig. 1d).

The elemental compositions of the nanoparticles were obtained using X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey scan spectrum in Fig. 2a indicates prominent peaks of C1s at 284eV and O1s peak at 532eV and N1s peak at 400eV. The percentage atomic concentration of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are 83.5%, 14.5% and 2%, respectively. The deconvoluted spectra of C1s (Fig. S1 a) indicate peaks of sp2 carbon (C–C/C=C) at 284.5eV, sp3 carbon C–N at 285.2eV and C=O at 288.2eV. Fig. S1 b is the deconvoluted spectra of O1s which have peak at 532.5eV. The high-resolution spectra of N1s was deconvoluted to pyrrolic (399.8eV) and graphitic nitrogen atoms (401eV) [40]. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum is included in the inset of Fig. 2a. This ratio of sp3/sp2 carbons (1.2) from XPS is calculated using the ratio of the integrated intensity of the peak at 284.5eV to the remaining peaks.

The surface functional groups present on the nanoparticles were studied using FTIR and is shown in Fig. 2b. The infra-red spectra revealed the presence of multiple surface passivated groups as follows here: The broad peak around 3400cm−1 arises due to stretching vibration of OH functional groups (phenolic/carboxyl), meanwhile the peak at 3232cm−1 can be assigned to N–H stretching vibrations. The small peaks at 2964cm−1, 2927cm−1, 2868cm−1 are due stretching vibration of CH3/CH2. The presence of carboxylic C=O and aromatic C=C stretching vibration is confirmed by 1716cm−1and 1628cm−1 respectively. The peak at 1456cm−1 occurs due to CH2 vibrations. The C–O–C stretching and CH bending vibrations are evident from the peaks at 1159cm−1 and at 991cm−1 respectively.

The structural purity of the carbon dots is analysed through Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum of the nanoparticles as given indicated in Fig. S2 has peaks around 1585cm− 1 (G-band) and D-band at 1350cm−1. G-band arises due to the in-plane bond stretching vibration of sp2 carbon atoms which is related to E2G mode of graphite [41]. D-band is forbidden in perfect graphite and become active in the presence of disorder. The 2D peak is absent in the Raman spectra which rules out the possibility of graphene quantum dots [42]. The integrated intensity ratio of D and G bands represents degree of disorder and functionalization (ratio of sp3/sp2 carbons) [43]. The ID/IG ratio of the carbon dots was calculated to be around 1.76, which indicates the synthesised nanoparticles have nanocrystalline graphitic structure.

In order to understand the formation mechanism of the carbon dots the FTIR spectra of the precursors and samples were collected at different stages of formation of the carbon nanoparticles; and are depicted in Fig. S3. In EDTA, the peak of C=O of acid group is around 1680cm−1. But in the FTIR spectra of sample collected during the formation state, a peak at 1745cm−1 appears; which is the characteristic peak of ester group [44,45]. Based on this observation the possible formation of carbon dots can be explained as follows. The EDTA and EG react to form an ester in presence of H3PO4, which is polymerised and further undergo carbonization (due to microwave irradiation), resulting in the formation of carbon dots. The possible synthesis mechanism schematically represented in Fig. S4.

The optical responses of the CDs were obtained in aqueous-dispersions using UV–vis spectroscopy and steady-state fluorescence. The UV–vis absorption spectrum as displayed in Fig. 3a, shows a shoulder around 240nm and an absorption tail which extends to visible region. The absorption shoulder may be due to π–π* transition of C=C in the carbon core.

The steady-state fluorescence data of the CDs after dispersing in ultrapure water, obtained at different excitation wavelengths are depicted in Fig. 3b. The PL emission spectra are broad, extending from blue to red colours, with excitation-wavelength independent emission behaviour. The full-width at half maximum (FWHM) at an excitation wavelength of 300nm was estimated to be about 155nm. Further increase in the excitation wavelength to 320nm and 350nm, the FWHM of the emission increased to 160nm and 165nm respectively. Meanwhile, the FWHM decreased to 150nm at an excitation wavelength of 370nm. The maximum PL intensity was obtained when excited at 350nm excitation, and the quantum yield of the carbon dots were measured to be about 16% (λ ex=350nm). The PL emission from the aqueous dispersion of the nanoparticles appears as white for naked eyes. The digital photographs corresponding to the aqueous-dispersion of CDs in sunlight and under UV-excitation are included in Fig. S5. As per several hypotheses proposed by earlier researchers, the inception of fluorescence in C-dots is due to defects in the organisation of their structures, mainly due to the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon clusters in definite ratios [46]. The decoration of the dangling bonds present on the surface of the CDs by functional groups, amides and carboxylic acids also plays a central role in destroying non-radiative energy traps (thereby generating electron-hole pairs) leading to fluorescence under UV light of a suitable wavelength [47].

The emission spectral component ratios were calculated by dividing the photoluminescence spectra into six colours (violet, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red) for different excitation wavelengths (300nm, 350nm and 370nm) and are represented as stack column in Fig. S6. At an excitation wavelength of 300nm the spectral component ratio of the blue-to-red intensity to the total intensity is 80%; meanwhile at 350nm the ratio decreased to 77%. On further increase in the excitation wavelength (370nm), the spectral composition ratio decreased to 76%. The quality of white light emission is estimated from the 1931 CIE (x, y) chromaticity coordinates using the PL data obtained for different excitation wavelengths. The chromaticity diagram showing the CIE colour parameters are shown in Fig. 3c. The colour parameters corresponding to excitation wavelengths of about 280nm, 300nm 350nm and 370nm are (0.23, 0.31), (0.24, 0.32), (0.24, 0.31) and (0.23, 0.30) respectively. The CIE coordinates obtained at all excitation wavelengths belong to the white gamut region, indicates that the aqueous dispersion of CDs emit white light under wide range of excitation wavelengths. The excitation spectra collected at the emission maximum (490nm) is included in Fig. S7. The peak at 240nm may corresponds to the absorption due to the C–C bonds in the carbon core, meanwhile the later ones may be related to the excitation of the defect centres due to surface functional groups [32].

In order to get further information about the photoluminescence mechanism time-resolved fluorescence of carbon dots were collected using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The time-resolved data was collected at 490nm, with an excitation wavelength of 340nm. The result of the TCSPS is presented in Fig. 3d. The PL decay is fitted using fourth exponential equation using DAS6 software (HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH). It have four life-time components:
τ

, with relative amplitudes

,

respectively. The average life-time is calculated using the equation,

35
ns
. The multiple life-times in the fluorescence decay imply the presence of various emissive species on the surface of the nanoparticles [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots as phosphors in white light emitting diodes
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous-dispersion of carbon dots developed in this work exhibit white light emission under UV excitation. But for device applications the solvent has to be removed, which generally results in the inhibition of fluorescence due to aggregation-induced quenching. Here we show that the CDs can be used for the production of white light emitting LED using remote-phosphor technology. This is realized by dispersing the CDs in a host matrix (PMMA), which is transparent to both excitation wavelengths and the visible emission range. The nanoparticle-dispersed PMMA matrix is further capped on to a 365nm UV-LED chip and upon biasing the UV-LED, white light emission is obtained due to the wavelength-conversion by the phosphor (CDs dispersed PMMA) from UV to visible. The emission spectrum was collected using a fibre-coupled spectrometer and is reported in Fig. 4a. The corresponding digital photograph of white LED is shown in the inset of Fig. 4a. The chromaticity diagram for the white LED, with the colour co-ordinates is depicted in Fig. 4b. The colour parameters corresponding to the white LED as obtained from the spectra emission are estimated to be CIE (0.34, 0.38), CRI of 84 and CCT of 5078K. By absorbing UV light from the chip, the polymer composite emit white light indicating the efficacy of CDs-PMMA composite as a white light converter.

In conclusion, we have synthesised white light emitting carbon dots utilising the esterification and polymerisation reaction between EDTA and ethylene glycol in the presence of H3PO4. The as-synthesised nanoparticles emit broad-band emission at single-wavelength UV excitation. The broadness of the emission spectra may be due to the presence of different functional groups or emissive sites on the surface of the nanoparticles. The presences of different emissive sites were confirmed by time resolved fluorescence (TCSPC) and FTIR spectroscopy. The carbon dots were dispersed in PMMA matrix and were demonstrated as a UV to visible range converter for white light emission, using remote-phosphor technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A Ph.D. grant to Julin Joseph is acknowledged to National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC). The authors thank to Dr. Lakshmi C. (Dept. of Chemistry, NITC) for providing facilities for fluorescence measurements, and Dr. P. Parameswaran for fruitful discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.05.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The supporting information contains physico-chemical characterisation details of CDs such as XPS, Raman, FTIR and spectral composition of the emission spectra, and excitation spectra.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic Light Emitting Materials and Devices
Organic Light-Emitting Diodes: Materials, Devices and Applications
Highly Efficient OLEDs with Phosphorescent Materials
